🎮 Micro:bit Project Blog

Maze Game

Tilt your Micro:bit to navigate a glowing dot through a maze!

By: Adrien B

Difficulty: Intermediate
Language: TypeScript — MakeCode
Hardware: BBC Micro:bit
Controls: Tilt & Logo

What Is It?

Maze Game turns the Micro:bit's 5×5 LED display into a tiny maze. The walls are drawn with bright LEDs, and you control a dimmer dot by physically tilting the Micro:bit in four directions. Navigate from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner to win — a duck emoji appears as your reward!

How to Play

1
The maze appears on startup When the Micro:bit starts, the maze walls light up on the LED display and your dot appears at the top-left corner (0, 0).
2
Tilt to move your dot Tilt right → move right. Tilt left → move left. Tilt forward (logo up) → move down. Tilt back (logo down) → move up.
3
Don't hit the walls! The dot can only move into empty spaces. Bright LEDs are walls — your dot will be blocked if you try to move into one.
4
Reach the goal to win Navigate to the bottom-right corner (position 4, 4). A duck icon appears, then the maze resets and you can play again!

Controls

↗️
Tilt RightMoves the dot one step to the right
↙️
Tilt LeftMoves the dot one step to the left
⬆️
Logo Up (tilt forward)Moves the dot one step down
⬇️
Logo Down (tilt back)Moves the dot one step up

Block 1 — On Start

This block runs once when the Micro:bit turns on. It draws the maze walls and places the player's dot at position (0, 0).

What each part does:

  • show leds — draws the maze layout. Lit LEDs are walls, dark LEDs are paths. The maze has a single route from top-left to bottom-right.
  • plot x (x) y (y) brightness 111 — places the player dot at coordinates (0, 0) — the top-left corner. Brightness 111 is dimmer than the walls (255), so you can tell them apart.

Block 2 — Tilt Controls (Four Directions)

There are four gesture blocks — one for each direction. They all work the same way: check if the move is valid, then move the dot. Here they all are:

What each part does:

  • on tilt right / left / logo up / logo down — each hat block listens for a specific physical tilt gesture. The Micro:bit's built-in accelerometer detects the tilt.
  • if x < 4 and point brightness = 0 then — a two-part safety check inside a C-shaped if block. First: make sure the dot won't go off the edge of the screen. Second: make sure the next position is empty (brightness 0 = no wall).
  • unplot x (x) y (y) — turns off the current dot position, erasing the dot from where it was.
  • change x by 1 (or -1, or change y) — updates the x or y variable to the new position. x controls left/right, y controls up/down.
  • plot x (x) y (y) brightness 111 — redraws the dot at the new position with the same dim brightness.

Block 3 — Forever (Win Condition)

This block runs in a continuous loop and checks every moment whether the player has reached the goal position.

What each part does:

  • forever — this loop runs continuously in the background, checking the win condition after every other block has had a chance to run.
  • if x = 4 and y = 4 then — checks whether the dot has reached the bottom-right corner, which is position (4, 4) on the 5×5 grid.
  • show icon 🦆 — displays the duck emoji across the whole LED grid as the win celebration!
  • pause (ms) 2000 — waits 2 seconds so the player can see their duck reward.
  • reset — restarts the Micro:bit, drawing the maze fresh so the player can try again.

The Completed Code (Blocks)

Here are all three blocks together — the full program as seen in MakeCode:

The three blocks work together: Block 1 sets up the maze, Block 2 handles movement on every tilt, and Block 3 constantly watches for the win condition in the background.

Key Coding Ideas Used

  • Gesture events — onGesture() detects physical tilting using the Micro:bit's accelerometer. No buttons needed!
  • Variables for position — x and y track where the dot is on the grid at all times. Updating them moves the dot.
  • Collision detection — led.pointBrightness() reads whether an LED is on. If it's a wall (brightness > 0), the move is blocked. Simple but clever!
  • Boundary checking — the x < 4 and x > 0 checks stop the dot from going off the edge of the display.
  • Brightness levels — walls use full brightness (set by show leds), but the player dot uses brightness 111 (dimmer) so you can tell them apart visually.
  • Forever loop for win detection — instead of checking inside each movement block, the win condition is checked continuously in a separate loop — cleaner code!

Try It Yourself — Run the Code Online

Open the MakeCode editor in your browser — no download needed. Create a new project and paste the JavaScript below, then switch to Blocks view.

🌐 MakeCode Micro:bit Editor
Open makecode.microbit.org ↗ Click New Project → paste the JavaScript below → switch to Blocks view

The Code in JavaScript

Copy and paste this into MakeCode's JavaScript tab — it will automatically convert to blocks!

maze_game.js 
let y = 0
let x = 0

input.onGesture(Gesture.TiltRight, function () {
    if (x < 4 && led.pointBrightness(x + 1, y) == 0) {
        led.unplot(x, y)
        x += 1
        led.plotBrightness(x, y, 111)
    }
})

input.onGesture(Gesture.TiltLeft, function () {
    if (x > 0 && led.pointBrightness(x - 1, y) == 0) {
        led.unplot(x, y)
        x += -1
        led.plotBrightness(x, y, 111)
    }
})

input.onGesture(Gesture.LogoUp, function () {
    if (y < 4 && led.pointBrightness(x, y + 1) == 0) {
        led.unplot(x, y)
        y += 1
        led.plotBrightness(x, y, 111)
    }
})

input.onGesture(Gesture.LogoDown, function () {
    if (y > 0 && led.pointBrightness(x, y - 1) == 0) {
        led.unplot(x, y)
        y += -1
        led.plotBrightness(x, y, 111)
    }
})

basic.showLeds(`
    . # . . .
    . # . # .
    . . . # .
    . # # # .
    . . . # .
    `)
led.plotBrightness(x, y, 111)

basic.forever(function () {
    if (x == 4 && y == 4) {
        basic.showIcon(IconNames.Duck)
        basic.pause(2000)
        control.reset()
    }
})
💡 Tip: After pasting, click the Blocks tab at the top of MakeCode to see your code turn into colourful blocks automatically!

Ideas to Improve It

Design your own maze layout by changing the pattern in the show leds block.
Add a timer — count how many seconds it takes to finish and try to beat your record.
Add multiple levels — after winning, load a harder maze instead of resetting.
Use radio to race a friend — both Micro:bits run the same maze at the same time.
Make the player dot blink so it's even easier to spot among the walls.
Maze Game — By: Adrien B
